nativeRange.habitat: Tropical mixed coral reefs, lagoons, and sand-and-rubble zones, almost always living commensally at the column or among the tentacles of tube anemones (Cerianthidae), carpet/branched anemones (Actinodendron, Actinostephanus), or occasionally slipper corals (Herpolitha). Often only the carapace shows while the rest of the crab stays tucked into the host.

decorPreferences: an established, stable reef system with mature live rock
a compatible host invertebrate (tube anemone, carpet anemone, or slipper coral) for the crab to shelter with
sand and rubble zones near the host where the crab can bury or hide
dim or shaded refuges; the crab tends to stay tucked into its host during the day

diet.notes: The Anemone Crab is an opportunistic carnivore that, in the wild, feeds on small prey and detritus captured by or drifting past its host anemone, and has been reported to nibble at the host's mucus and tissues. In captivity, hobbyist reports describe it accepting meaty foods such as mysis, frozen brine shrimp, finely chopped clam, mussel, and small pieces of marine fish, as well as sinking carnivore pellets. Targeted feeding delivered directly to the host area is generally advised so the crab is not outcompeted by faster tankmates.

goodWith: a compatible tube anemone (Cerianthus sp.) or large carpet/branched anemone host
peaceful medium-sized reef fish that do not pick at small crabs
established LPS and soft corals (with adequate feeding)
reef-safe snails and similarly sized hermit crabs
avoidWith: small ornamental shrimp and very small fish that could be ambushed at night
wrasses, hawkfish, triggerfish, pufferfish, and other known crab/shrimp predators
other Anemone Crabs in smaller systems (intraspecific aggression has been reported)
cylindrical/tube anemones in undersized tanks where the crab can damage the host
any system that has been treated with copper-based medications

summary: The Anemone Crab is a small, boldly patterned swimming crab from the Indo-Pacific that spends most of its life tucked into tube anemones, carpet anemones, or slipper corals. Seldom seen in the trade, captive husbandry data is limited, making it a species best suited to experienced reef keepers.
careNotes:
The Anemone Crab (Lissocarcinus laevis) is a small, vividly patterned member of the swimming-crab family Portunidae, commonly encountered by divers across the Indo-Pacific tucked into the base of tube anemones (family Cerianthidae), large branched or carpet anemones such as Actinodendron and Actinostephanus, and occasionally on slipper corals such as Herpolitha. The carapace patterning is highly variable — typically reddish-brown to white with bold blotches — which accounts for the alternative common name 'Harlequin Crab'. It is unrelated to the porcelain anemone crab Neopetrolisthes maculatus, a different family altogether that filter-feeds alongside clownfish in host anemones.

Trade availability is limited, and documented long-term captive successes are relatively few, so the Anemone Crab is generally considered a species for experienced reef keepers rather than beginners. Where captive keeping has been reported, the consistent success factors are a stable, well-established reef system, a compatible host invertebrate for the crab to shelter in, and regular targeted feeding of meaty foods — mussel, clam, fish, or mysis — delivered close to the host so the crab is not outcompeted by faster tankmates. The species tends to stay concealed in its host during the day, with only the carapace visible.

Like most portunid swimming crabs, the Anemone Crab is an opportunistic carnivore and has been reported to nip at the host anemone or take small motile tankmates, particularly in cramped setups or when underfed. It is generally considered reef-safe with caution. Like all marine invertebrates, it is sensitive to copper-based medications, which must never be used in a tank where it is kept. Stable salinity and trace elements — including iodine — are commonly advised to support healthy molting.
breedingNotes: The Anemone Crab has not been bred in captivity as far as available records show. As with other portunid swimming crabs, females are expected to brood eggs attached to the pleopods (the small appendages on the underside of the abdomen) and release planktonic zoea larvae that pass through multiple developmental stages before settling. Rearing these larvae in a home system would face the same challenges encountered with almost all marine crustaceans — keeping the tiny larvae fed with appropriately sized prey while preventing them from being drawn into filters or skimmers. No documented captive breeding protocol exists for this species.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied primary source. Site searches and category browsing did not return a product page for Lissocarcinus laevis / Anemone Crab / Harlequin Crab at the time of research — the species is not currently in their catalog. No primary data extracted from this source; fell back to the species-specific sources listed under additional[].

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Manifest-supplied secondary. No product page returned for Lissocarcinus laevis at the time of research; the species is not currently in their catalog. Documented as a failed lookup per the playbook fallback rule.

additional.name: Reeflings species care guide
additional.notes: Effective primary husbandry source: Indo-Pacific reefs/lagoons, max 5 cm (2 in), 24–28 °C (75–82 °F), pH 8.0–8.5, SG 1.023–1.026, 50 L (13 gal) minimum, care level moderate-to-easy, carnivore, peaceful temperament, reef-safe with cautions about preying on small organisms if underfed, symbiotic with sea anemones.

additional.name: Reefex (Reeflex)
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Portunidae, Decapoda), distribution (Indo-Pacific including Andaman/Nicobar, Australia, Hawaii, Madagascar, Philippines, Japan, Sri Lanka, etc.), max size ~3 cm, depth 9–82 m, temperature 26–29 °C, diet (mysis, brine shrimp, clam, pellets, flake, etc.). Also notes 'no reports of successful long-term captive keeping yet' and that the crab can damage cylindrical anemones in captivity.

additional.name: Mantis Reefs product page
additional.notes: Trade-source secondary. Care level moderate, temperament aggressive, reef-safe with caution, may pose a threat to other inverts and small fish; seller reports successfully pairing one with a Bubble Tip Anemone in captivity and feeding it mussels/clams/whole fish.

additional.name: Crustaceology / JungleDragon / Lembeh Resort critter log
additional.notes: Field/observation sources used for ecology: body size 3–4 cm, depth to ~30 m, mixed reef and sand-rubble habitats, commensal with tube anemones (Cerianthidae), Actinodendron, Actinostephanus haeckeli, and occasionally slipper corals (Herpolitha). Clarifies separation from L. orbicularis (the sea-cucumber commensal) and from Neopetrolisthes maculatus (the porcelain 'Anemone Crab').

crustacean.moltingNotes: Molting frequency has not been well documented for this species. As with other small portunid swimming crabs, healthy adults are expected to molt their shell periodically — likely on a timescale of weeks to a couple of months — and to remain soft and vulnerable for roughly one to two days afterward while the new shell hardens. Stable water parameters, particularly salinity, calcium, magnesium, and iodine and trace-element levels, are commonly advised to support successful molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding. Expected to follow the standard portunid pattern of pleopod-brooded eggs and planktonic zoea larvae that are very difficult to rear in standard reef systems.
